user_steers.steer: Trace the historical development of LLMs, from their origins to current models capable of human-like conversation.
Investigate the impact of the Transformer architecture on language models and the shift it caused in the field of AI, contrasting the state of AI around 2010 with the post-Transformer era.
In-depth technical explanation of LLMs and the Transformer architecture, including their underlying mechanisms and mathematical foundations.
Mathematical foundations of the Transformer architecture, including Self-Attention, Multi-Head Attention, and Positional Encoding.
Focus solely on the mathematical aspects of the Transformer architecture and related concepts.
Continue focusing on the mathematical aspects of the Transformer architecture, including Scaled Dot-Product Attention, Multi-Head Attention, and Positional Encoding.
Synthesize the power-law equations for LLM scaling, comparing the Kaplan (2020) and Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) constants.

Continue focusing on the mathematical aspects of the Transformer architecture, including Scaled Dot-Product Attention, Multi-Head Attention, and Positional Encoding; Synthesize the power-law equations for LLM scaling, comparing the Kaplan (2020) and Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) constants; Address the gaps: Mathematical proof that PE(pos+k) can be represented as a linear transformation of PE(pos) for sinusoidal encodings; Specific numerical constants (alpha, beta, A, B, E) and the exact loss function formulations for Kaplan (2020) vs. Chinchilla (2022); Formal matrix dimensionality mapping for Multi-Head Attention, specifically the rank-consistency of the concatenation and output projection (W^O).

Continue focusing on extracting scaling law constants, proving the linear shift property of sinusoidal Positional Encodings, and performing dimensionality/rank-consistency analysis of Multi-Head Attention.

options_presented.text: Extract and synthesize the specific scaling law constants (A, B, alpha, beta, E) from the Kaplan and Hoffmann papers.
Derive the mathematical proof for the sinusoidal positional encoding linear shift property, identifying the rotation matrix M.
Perform a formal dimensionality and rank-consistency analysis of the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) projection phase.

outputs_written.topic: Continue focusing on extracting scaling law constants, proving the linear shift property of sinusoidal Positional Encodings, and performing dimensionality/rank-consistency analysis of Multi-Head Attention.
outputs_written.text: The empirical framework established by Kaplan et al. demonstrates that neural language model performance is governed by power-law relationships between model size, dataset scale, and compute [53], [55]. A critical finding is the superior sample efficiency of larger architectures, which suggests that optimal compute allocation favors training massive models on relatively modest datasets and terminating training well before convergence [56]. gaps: The specific block-diagonal rotation matrix M such that PE(pos+k) = M * PE(pos) for sinusoidal encodings.
Exact numerical values for alpha, beta, A, B, and E (irreducible loss) comparing Kaplan (2020) and Chinchilla (2022) formulations.
Formal tensor dimensionality mapping for Multi-Head Attention to verify rank-consistency during the concatenation and WO projection phase.
